items.title: Detect Ollama availability and installed models via the tags endpoint
items.description: The orchestrator probes the local Ollama tags endpoint to learn whether the runtime is up and which models are actually installed, caching the result. Routing decisions consider only models that are confirmed present; a configured-but-absent model is treated as unavailable rather than assumed.
items.applicability: Startup capability discovery and any decision to route a request to a specific model.
items.example: A probe of /api/tags returns the installed model list; if qwen2.5-coder:14b is absent, code-review requests fall back rather than calling a model that is not there.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: cache the probe result and refresh it on a background timer, never probe synchronously on the request path. Test implication: assert routing treats a missing model as unavailable and that a failed probe degrades cleanly. Generator implication: generated features check the cached availability before dispatching. Honest boundary: the tags list confirms a model is installed, not that a given call will fit in memory or succeed, so per-run budgets and OOM avoidance still apply.

items.title: Detect likely GPU availability as a best-effort signal, never a hard dependency
items.description: The orchestrator may gather a best-effort signal that a GPU is probably present (for example from the runtime's own reporting or a lightweight environment check) and use it only to inform scheduling, such as how many heavy calls to allow concurrently. It must never require a GPU, and behaviour must be correct whether the signal says present, absent, or unknown.
items.applicability: Scheduling heuristics and capacity planning for heavy model calls.
items.example: A likely-GPU signal lets the orchestrator permit a slightly larger run queue; on a CPU-only host the same code runs with a smaller queue and longer timeouts.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: treat the GPU signal as advisory input to scheduling, with safe defaults when it is unknown. Test implication: assert all logic passes with the signal forced to absent and to unknown. Generator implication: generated orchestration never branches into a GPU-required path. Honest boundary: GPU detection is heuristic and can be wrong about model, memory, or whether the runtime will actually use the GPU, so it must not gate correctness.

items.title: Route code-focused work to the coder model
items.description: Tasks centred on code, such as code review, bug explanation, refactoring, and template review, route to a coding-specialised model (for example qwen2.5-coder:14b). The role policy maps task kinds to model roles so the same kind of work consistently reaches a model suited to it.
items.applicability: Selecting a model for a given task kind.
items.example: A bug-explanation request is routed to the coder role; a template-review request goes to the same role with a code-oriented prompt.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: encode the task-kind-to-role map as data, with a fallback role when the preferred model is absent. Test implication: assert each code task kind resolves to the coder role and degrades when it is unavailable. Generator implication: generated callers state a task kind, not a model name, so routing stays centralised. Honest boundary: a coder model is suited to code work but is not authoritative; its output is still reviewed and the deterministic fallback remains the source of truth.

items.title: Route long-form reasoning to the reasoning model with a larger token budget
items.description: Tasks like architecture planning, risk and gap analysis, and other long-form reasoning route to a reasoning-specialised model (for example deepseek-r1:14b). Such a model spends tokens on hidden intermediate reasoning, so the orchestrator grants it a larger token budget than a coder-style call to avoid truncating its answer.
items.applicability: Selecting a model and budget for analytical, planning, or open-ended reasoning tasks.
items.example: An architecture-risk analysis routes to the reasoning role with a generously sized output budget, so the model's internal reasoning does not crowd out the final conclusion.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: attach a per-role default token budget, larger for reasoning roles, and make it configurable. Test implication: assert reasoning tasks request a larger budget than coder tasks. Generator implication: generated planning flows pick the reasoning role and inherit its budget. Honest boundary: a larger budget costs more time and memory and does not guarantee a better answer; the output is still non-deterministic and governed by review.

items.title: Treat the embedding model as an optional retrieval aid
items.description: An embedding model supports optional semantic retrieval over stored knowledge and code. It is not required for the platform to function; when it or its vector store is absent, retrieval falls back to deterministic keyword or structured queries and the system still operates.
items.applicability: Semantic search and retrieval-augmented prompt construction.
items.example: With embeddings available, a query retrieves semantically similar knowledge; without them, the same query uses a keyword and metadata search and the feature still returns results.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: gate embedding use behind availability and provide a deterministic retrieval fallback. Test implication: assert retrieval works with embeddings disabled. Generator implication: generated retrieval never hard-depends on a vector store. Honest boundary: keyword fallback is less semantically aware than embeddings, so retrieval quality is reduced but not broken when embeddings are off.

items.title: Allow new model roles to be added without code branching
items.description: The role policy is open to future models (such as additional coder, reasoning, or reranking models) by adding a data-driven role entry that maps task kinds to a model and budget. New capabilities slot in through configuration and review rather than scattering model names through the codebase.
items.applicability: Evolving the model line-up over time without destabilising callers.
items.example: Introducing a reranker role adds a role entry and a fallback; existing callers that request reranking begin using it, or degrade if it is absent, without per-call code changes.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: keep the role table and its fallbacks as reviewed configuration validated at startup. Test implication: assert an unknown or unavailable role resolves to a defined fallback rather than failing. Generator implication: generators target roles and task kinds, easing portability as models change. Honest boundary: adding a role makes it selectable, not validated; each new model still needs its own evaluation before it is trusted for a task.

items.title: Serialize heavy calls through a run queue
items.description: Heavy model calls go through a run queue that bounds how many execute at once, rather than being launched directly from request handlers. Serializing heavy work protects limited GPU or CPU memory, keeps the host responsive, and prevents a burst of requests from overwhelming the runtime.
items.applicability: Admission control for all model inference requests.
items.example: Three simultaneous architecture-analysis requests enter the queue; with a concurrency of one for heavy reasoning, they run in turn instead of thrashing memory.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: enforce the queue in the orchestrator so no caller can bypass it, and surface queue position or wait state to the UI. Test implication: assert excess heavy requests queue rather than running concurrently. Generator implication: generated callers submit to the queue and await admission. Honest boundary: queuing trades latency for stability, so very latency-sensitive work may need a separate, smaller-model fast path rather than the heavy queue.

items.title: Bound every run with a token budget and a timeout
items.description: Each model run carries an explicit maximum token budget and a wall-clock timeout owned by the orchestrator. A run that exceeds either is stopped and handled as a failure, so no single call can consume unbounded time or memory.
items.example: A reasoning call with a large but finite token budget and a generous timeout is aborted if it runs past the timeout, returning a typed failure the caller can handle.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: pass both limits to the runtime where supported and additionally enforce the timeout on the orchestrator side. Test implication: assert a run exceeding the timeout is aborted and reported, not left hanging. Generator implication: generated calls always carry or inherit both bounds. Honest boundary: token and time limits cap resource use but can truncate a legitimate long answer, so budgets must be tuned per role, not set arbitrarily low.

items.title: Emit telemetry for every model run
items.description: Each run records telemetry: the model and role used, elapsed time, token counts where available, and whether it succeeded or failed and why. This telemetry drives capacity tuning, surfaces slow or failing models, and feeds the audit trail without exposing prompt contents in the metrics stream.
items.applicability: Performance monitoring, capacity planning, and reliability tracking.
items.example: A telemetry record shows role=reasoning, model=deepseek-r1:14b, elapsed=42s, status=ok, helping an operator see that reasoning calls are the heaviest.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: keep metrics free of sensitive prompt text; correlate to the audit log by id instead. Test implication: assert every run, success or failure, emits a telemetry record. Generator implication: generated calls are automatically measured. Honest boundary: telemetry reveals cost and reliability, not answer quality, which requires separate evaluation.

items.title: Split an over-large prompt and retry smaller on failure
items.description: When a call fails because the prompt is too large for the model or memory, the orchestrator reduces scope, by chunking the input, trimming low-value context, or summarising, and retries with a smaller prompt rather than repeatedly sending the same oversized request.
items.applicability: Recovery from context-length and memory-pressure failures.
items.example: A code-review request over a huge file that fails is retried per-section, and the section results are combined, rather than re-sending the whole file unchanged.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: detect size or memory failures specifically and shrink the prompt deterministically before retrying. Test implication: assert an oversized prompt triggers a smaller retry, not an identical resend. Generator implication: generated long-input flows are chunk-aware. Honest boundary: splitting can lose cross-section context, so combined results may be less coherent than a single-pass answer and may need a final reconciling step.

items.title: Bound retries; never loop forever
items.description: Retries are capped at a small fixed count with backoff. After the cap the orchestrator gives up, returns a typed failure, and lets the deterministic fallback take over. The system never enters an unbounded retry loop against a model that keeps failing.
items.applicability: The failure-handling path of every model call.
items.example: A call that fails three times with backoff stops retrying and returns a failure; the feature proceeds with its non-model fallback.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: count retries per logical request and apply backoff, distinguishing retryable from non-retryable errors. Test implication: assert a persistently failing model causes a bounded number of attempts and then a clean failure. Generator implication: generated callers never implement their own unbounded retry. Honest boundary: a low cap can give up on a transient issue that one more attempt would have cleared, so the cap and backoff should be tuned, not removed.

items.title: Avoid GPU out-of-memory and overrun loops
items.description: The orchestrator actively avoids patterns that thrash a GPU into out-of-memory or repeated overrun: it limits concurrent heavy models, avoids loading multiple large models at once, treats an OOM signal as a non-retryable-as-is failure that triggers scope reduction, and prefers serializing over piling on more load.
items.applicability: Memory-pressure management on constrained local hardware.
items.example: An OOM on a reasoning call does not trigger an immediate identical retry; instead the orchestrator reduces the prompt or waits for the queue to drain before trying again.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: classify OOM distinctly and respond by shrinking or serializing, not by hammering the same call. Test implication: assert an OOM does not cause an immediate identical retry loop. Generator implication: generated flows respect the single-heavy-model-at-a-time discipline. Honest boundary: without reliable hardware telemetry the orchestrator infers memory pressure from failures rather than measuring it, so its avoidance is reactive and best-effort.

items.title: The deterministic fallback remains the authority
items.description: Model output augments deterministic logic; it never replaces it as the authority. Every model-assisted feature has a defined deterministic path that runs when models are unavailable or fail, and that path, not the model, is the source of truth for the system's behaviour.
items.applicability: Every feature that consults a local model.
items.example: A model suggests a refactor, but the deterministic validation, build, and tests decide whether it is accepted; with no model present the feature still functions on its deterministic path.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: implement and test the deterministic path first, then layer the model as an enhancement. Test implication: assert the feature behaves correctly with all models disabled. Generator implication: generated features always ship a model-free path. Honest boundary: a deterministic fallback may be less capable or convenient than the model-assisted path, but it guarantees the system keeps working and stays governable.

items.title: Core behaviour and tests must not require a GPU or Ollama
items.description: The application's core behaviour and its automated test suite must pass with no GPU and no Ollama present. Model orchestration is an optional capability layered on a system that is complete without it, consistent with the platform's local-first, MSSQL-only baseline.
items.applicability: Build agents, CI, and any minimal deployment.
items.example: On a CPU-only build server with no Ollama, the full test suite is green and every core page loads, because model calls are gated behind availability and fall back.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: gate all model calls behind the cached availability check and never call the runtime synchronously on the request path. Test implication: a model-free environment must produce a fully passing run. Generator implication: generated tests never depend on a model being installed. Honest boundary: model-specific behaviour cannot be exercised without the model, so those paths need separate, clearly optional integration tests that are skipped when the runtime is absent.

items.title: Capture prompt and response to an audit log
items.description: Each model run records the prompt sent and the response received (with secrets redacted and size capped) to an audit log correlated to the originating request and telemetry record. This makes model-assisted decisions reproducible and reviewable after the fact.
items.applicability: Auditability and debugging of model-assisted features.
items.example: A refactor suggestion's exact prompt and response are retrievable from the audit log by run id, so a reviewer can see why the model proposed what it did.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: redact secrets and cap stored size, and protect the audit store like other sensitive data. Test implication: assert each run writes a correlated, redacted audit entry. Generator implication: generated calls are audited automatically. Honest boundary: prompts and responses can be large and sensitive, so retention and access must be governed; the audit log records what happened, not whether the answer was correct.

items.title: Treat model output as non-deterministic and review-gated
items.description: Local model output varies between runs and can be wrong or fabricated. The orchestrator treats every model result as a suggestion that must be validated, by deterministic checks, tests, or human review, before it affects durable state or installed knowledge. The model is never the final arbiter.
items.applicability: Any consumption of model output by a downstream process.
items.example: A model-generated code change is run through build and tests; a model-suggested knowledge item enters the proposal queue for human approval rather than being installed directly.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: route model output through validation or the approval lifecycle before it becomes authoritative. Test implication: assert model output cannot enter approved knowledge or durable state without passing a gate. Generator implication: generated flows always validate model output. Honest boundary: governance reduces the risk of acting on a wrong or fabricated answer but cannot make a non-deterministic model deterministic; variability remains and must be expected.

items.title: Probe model health off the request path and cache the result
items.description: Availability and health of the model runtime are probed by a background monitor on a timer, and the result is cached. Controllers and views read the cached snapshot; they never call the runtime synchronously while handling a request, which would risk hanging the page if the runtime is slow or down.
items.applicability: All request-path code that needs to know whether models are usable.
items.example: A page that offers a model-assisted action reads the cached availability flag to decide whether to show it, instead of calling the runtime inline and blocking the render.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: read health from a cached snapshot service, refreshed in the background, exactly as other optional services are handled. Test implication: assert no controller or view calls the runtime synchronously and that a down runtime never blocks a page. Generator implication: generated UI gates model features on the cached flag. Honest boundary: a cached snapshot can be briefly stale, so a call may still fail after the snapshot said available, which the bounded-retry and fallback rules then handle.
